result:
The summer I learned to drive stick, the world shrank to the size of my left foot.

The car was my uncle’s ’94 Honda Civic, a pale blue that used to be bright but had been sanded down by sun and time into something closer to memory than paint. The fabric on the ceiling sagged just enough to brush your hair, and the AC either blew arctic wind or nothing at all, depending on how merciful it felt. It smelled like old coffee, gasoline, and the lemon-scented hanging tree that had long since given up but still swung from the rearview.

My uncle believed that if you couldn’t drive a manual, you couldn’t really drive. He also believed oil changes were optional and seatbelts were a suggestion, so his credibility was mixed. But he had the car and the time, and I had the strange, swelling sense of urgency that sits behind your ribs at seventeen and whispers now, now, now at every opportunity.

On the first day, he took me to the top of the steepest hill in town.

“Why here?” I asked, staring down the incline. The road seemed to fall away like a sentence that forgot how to end.

“Because if you can start here, you can start anywhere.”

This sounded like the kind of thing people say before montage music starts and you see a quick-cut series of failures ending in triumph. Real life, as it turned out, has fewer musical cues and more burning clutch smell.

He had me stall the car dutyfully twenty-three times in a row. I counted. Each time, some combination of physics and panic collided in my left leg, and I’d pop the clutch or feather the gas wrong. The engine would lurch and die, the car would roll back an alarming six inches, and my heart would attempt to escape through my neck.

People lined up behind us, a congregation of minor inconveniences in sedans and SUVs. No one honked. I expected symphonies of rage, operas of irritation. I was prepared for it, braced for it, resented it preemptively. But the line of cars waited, patient and distant, as though I were underwater and everything behind me existed one layer removed.

“Again,” my uncle said. He was chewing sunflower seeds and spitting the shells into an empty coffee cup. With every stall, I became more convinced I would never be able to do something as straightforward as making a car move forward. The future rearranged itself briefly: maybe I would live in this car forever, halfway up this hill, subsisting on fast-food bags and irony.

On the twenty-fourth try, my foot found the exact pace of the engine’s request. The pedal rose under my toes like it wanted to go. I fed it gas, held my breath, and suddenly the world agreed with me. The car moved, smooth as a thought you’re sure of.

“That,” my uncle said, “was it. Now don’t forget it.”

He said it like you can choose not to forget things. Like memory isn’t a river knocking pieces of you loose as it goes.

—

I did forget it, of course, just like I forget everything mechanical the moment I’m not using it. But the feeling stuck: the tiny miracle when something complicated becomes simple in the body; when what you need from the world and what the world requires in return line up, however briefly.

Years later, standing in a grocery store aisle, I found that same feeling during the quiet middle of a Tuesday. I was staring at two nearly identical jars of pasta sauce, red commas on a shelf that never ended, when a kid in the next aisle started crying—not the shrill wail of small injustices, but a low, rumbling sob that had the weight of something far too large for a body that size.

I turned the corner in time to see him lying on his back on the polished floor, tiny hands fisted, screaming at the ceiling. His mother knelt beside him, shoulders tense, eyes shining with the glassy look of someone at the border of public shame and private overwhelm. People were doing that careful, polite ignoring you only see in supermarkets and elevators—everyone determined not to have a face about it.

I caught the mother’s eye for half a second. There was no message exchanged, no wordless telepathy. Just a flicker of her noticing that I’d noticed, and bracing for judgment.

My hands were still on the cart handle. I had no idea what to do, and I wasn’t responsible for doing anything. I could have walked on. It would’ve been the reasonable, efficient, adult thing to do.

Instead, I crouched down at the end of the aisle, still a few feet away, and lay down on my back on the floor.

The tiles were cooler than I expected. Fluorescent lights hummed above me like a nest of electric bees. From this angle, the towering shelves felt absurd—vast architecture for cereal and canned soup. A cart squeaked somewhere behind my head.

The little boy’s crying stuttered, then paused, like someone had pulled the tape from the cassette player but left it running. I stared at the ceiling, hands folded loosely over my stomach, trying not to make this about anything. Not rescue. Not performance. Just: I am also on the floor. That is all.

After a moment, socks appeared in my peripheral vision: tiny, blue, slightly worn at the heel. The kid shuffled over so he was lying parallel to me, about a foot away. His breathing was still ragged, but the edge had gone out of it.

“Hi,” he said.

“Hi,” I said.

We stared up together. One of the fluorescent bulbs flickered with quiet desperation.

“That one is broken,” he informed me.

“Yeah,” I said. “It’s trying really hard, though.”

He considered this. “My mom says I can’t have the dinosaur cereal.”

I turned my head. He was four, maybe five. His face was wet, eyes huge.

“Do you want the dinosaur cereal,” I asked, “or do you want the feeling of getting what you want?”

He made a face. “What?”

“Sometimes I think I’m mad about the thing,” I said, searching for words. “But I’m actually mad about not being the person who gets to decide.”

He frowned at the ceiling, like maybe there were answers hidden between the light panels.

“I want both,” he said, finally.

“Yeah,” I said. “Me too.”

We lay there a little longer. After a while, his mother’s shoes appeared. She looked at me, not quite sure how to arrange her face.

“Sorry,” she said.

“For what?” I asked, still on the floor.

“Everything,” she said, and we both laughed, because what else could you do with a sentence like that?

The boy stood up when she asked him to. There was no apology, no moral, no neat resolution. They walked away, cart rattling. I stayed there for another minute, letting the cool of the floor sink into my spine.

When I finally stood, my leg had pins and needles from the awkward angle, like blood coming back to a limb you forgot you owned.

—

The thing no one tells you about growing older is how much of it is relearning things you knew instinctively as a child and then had trained out of you: lie down when you’re tired. Cry when you’re overwhelmed. Laugh when something is funny even if no one else is laughing. Ask why more often than is socially comfortable.

At twelve, I built an entire city out of cardboard and tape in our garage. There were streets and alleys, windows drawn in marker, a river made from the blue side of old cereal boxes. I spent hours moving Lego people through it, narrating their lives in whispers: here is the baker who wants to be a poet but is afraid of hunger; here is the mechanic who loves quiet, intricate things; here are the kids who skate down the middle of the night.

No one ever lived there but me, and I dismantled it eventually for lack of space and tolerance from adults who needed the garage for cars. But some part of my brain never stopped walking those streets. I still find myself arranging invisible cardboard, building little worlds out of schedules and spreadsheets and conversations, hoping they’ll be livable.

Sometimes, late at night, when the house is quiet and the day has softened enough to let thoughts through, I think about how much of life is just managing the distance between what we thought it would be and what it is. The gap isn’t a failure; it’s where all the texture lives. The jokes, the wrong turns, the stubborn persistence of wanting dinosaur cereal in a universe that mostly stocks bran flakes.

If there’s any single thread that ties these vignettes together—the stalling car, the grocery store floor, the cardboard city—it’s this: we are always, in one way or another, learning how to start on a hill.

Your hill might be a conversation you don’t know how to begin, a project you’re afraid to touch, a loss that rearranged your insides so completely you’re not sure what language you speak now. The miracle isn’t that some people sail up the incline effortlessly. It’s that anyone, ever, manages to coordinate all the moving parts inside and out, to find that delicate balance between letting go and holding on just enough.

Foot on the clutch. Hand on the brake. Heart thudding. People behind you, or not. The road ahead, or not. And then, suddenly, the exact moment where the car, the hill, the noise, your fear, and your desire to move all tip in the same direction.

You ease your foot up.

You give it a little gas.

You don’t know if you’ll stall until you don’t.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
